sanskrit_line: klaibyaṁ mā sma gamaḥ pārtha naitat tvayy upapadyate
translation: Yield not to impotence, O Arjuna, son of Pritha! It does not befit thee. Cast off this mean weakness of the heart. Stand up, O scorcher of foes!
in_plain_terms: Sometimes the kindest thing someone can tell you is: enough. You've sat with the pain, you've honored it, and now it's time to get up. Self-pity feels like processing, but past a certain point it's just hiding.
stoic_parallel_quote: How does it help, my husband, to make misfortune heavier by complaining about it? This is more fit for a king: to seize your adversities head on.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca deliver the same tough-love message: there's a point where mourning becomes a way to avoid doing what you know needs doing.
action_step: Think of one thing you've been avoiding because it feels hard. Open it. Just look at it. You don't have to finish it today.
reflection_prompt: What are you calling 'patience' that might actually be avoidance?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: aśocyān anvaśocas tvaṁ prajñā-vādāṁś ca bhāṣase
translation: Thou hast grieved for those that should not be grieved for, yet thou speakest words of wisdom. The wise grieve neither for the living nor for the dead.
in_plain_terms: You've been making a very convincing case for the wrong side. The arguments are solid, your logic is sharp, but you already know the conclusion is off. That's the hardest kind of self-deception to catch, because it sounds so reasonable.
stoic_parallel_quote: It is impossible for a man to begin to learn that which he thinks that he knows.
stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Epictetus call out the same trap: sounding wise while avoiding what you actually know to be true.
action_step: Pick one opinion you've been defending lately. Ask yourself honestly: am I arguing for this because it's true, or because it's comfortable?
reflection_prompt: Where are you using good logic to protect a bad conclusion?

sanskrit_line: mātrā-sparśās tu kaunteya śītoṣṇa-sukha-duḥkha-dāḥ
translation: The contacts of the senses with the objects, O son of Kunti, which cause heat and cold and pleasure and pain, have a beginning and an end; they are impermanent; endure them bravely, O Arjuna!
in_plain_terms: That feeling you have right now? It has an expiration date. It showed up, and it will leave. This is true of the terrible days and, honestly, the great ones too. Your job isn't to stop the weather. It's to stop pretending the weather is permanent.
stoic_parallel_quote: To bear trials with a calm mind robs misfortune of its strength and burden.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca agree: bearing difficulty calmly isn't just noble. It actually takes the teeth out of the difficulty itself.
action_step: Next time a bad mood hits, set a timer for 90 minutes. Check if it still feels the same when the timer goes off.
reflection_prompt: What discomfort are you treating as permanent that's actually just passing through?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: yaṁ hi na vyathayanty ete puruṣaṁ puruṣarṣabha
translation: That firm man whom surely these afflict not, O chief among men, to whom pleasure and pain are the same, is fit for attaining immortality!
in_plain_terms: The person who stays steady when things get bad and doesn't get giddy when things go well isn't numb. They've just stopped handing the remote control to their circumstances. That steadiness isn't a personality trait. It's a skill you build.
stoic_parallel_quote: Many are harmed by fear itself, and many may have come to their fate while dreading fate.
stoic_bridge: Both traditions point to the same insight: it's rarely the pain itself that breaks you, but the flinching before it arrives.
action_step: The next time something goes really well today, notice how quickly you start worrying about losing it. Just notice.
reflection_prompt: When was the last time your fear of something turned out to be worse than the thing itself?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: nāsato vidyate bhāvo nābhāvo vidyate sataḥ
translation: The unreal hath no being; there is no non-being of the Real; the truth about both has been seen by the knowers of the Truth (or the seers of the Essence).
in_plain_terms: Most of what's stressing you out right now isn't real. It's a story you built on top of what actually happened. Someone didn't reply to your email. That's the fact. 'They hate my work and I'm going to get fired' is the fiction. Learning to tell the difference is basically a superpower.
stoic_parallel_quote: Keep thyself to the first bare and naked apprehensions of things, as they present themselves unto thee, and add not unto them. It is reported unto thee, that such a one speaketh ill of thee. Well; that he speaketh ill of thee, so much is reported. But that thou art hurt thereby, is not reported: that is the addition of opinion, which thou must exclude.
stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Marcus say the same thing: stick to what's actually in front of you and stop adding your own narration on top.
action_step: Take whatever's bothering you right now and strip it down to just the facts. No interpretation, no prediction. What actually happened?
reflection_prompt: What story are you telling yourself right now that you've confused with reality?

sanskrit_line: vāsāṁsi jīrṇāni yathā vihāya navāni gṛhṇāti naro 'parāṇi
translation: Just as a man casts off worn-out clothes and puts on new ones, so also the embodied Self casts off worn-out bodies and enters others that are new.
in_plain_terms: Change isn't happening to you. Change is how everything works. That career you outgrew, that relationship that ended, that version of yourself you can't recognize anymore. You didn't fail those things. You just changed clothes.
stoic_parallel_quote: Both the substances themselves, we see as a flood, are in a continual flux; and all actions in a perpetual change; and the causes themselves, subject to a thousand alterations, neither is there anything almost, that may ever be said to be now settled and constant.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus see change not as a disruption of life but as the basic operating principle of everything that exists.
action_step: Think of one thing from your past that felt like a devastating ending. Notice how it turned out to be a door.
reflection_prompt: What are you holding onto right now that you've already outgrown?

sanskrit_line: sva-dharmam api cāvekṣya na vikampitum arhasi
translation: Further, having regard to thy own duty, thou shouldst not waver, for there is nothing higher for a Kshatriya than a righteous war.
in_plain_terms: You know what you need to do. You've known for a while. The problem isn't information. It's nerve. Every day you spend 'thinking about it' is a day you're choosing hesitation over the thing that's clearly yours to do.
stoic_parallel_quote: It's not at all that we have too short a time to live, but that we squander a great deal of it.
stoic_parallel_source: Seneca, On the Brevity of Life, 1.3-4a
stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Seneca say the same thing: you have enough time. You're just spending it on hesitation.
action_step: Name the thing you've been putting off. Write down exactly one next step. Do that step before lunch.
reflection_prompt: What decision have you already made in your gut that your mind keeps pretending is still open?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: sukha-duḥkhe same kṛtvā lābhālābhau jayājayau
translation: Having made pleasure and pain, gain and loss, victory and defeat the same, engage thou in battle for the sake of battle; thus thou shalt not incur sin.
in_plain_terms: Do the thing because the thing needs doing, not because you'll get praised for it. Before the meeting, the pitch, the hard conversation, find your center. The outcome isn't yours to control. The quality of your engagement is.
stoic_parallel_quote: Keep in mind that it isn't the one who has it in for you and takes a swipe that harms you, but rather the harm comes from your own belief about the abuse. So when someone arouses your anger, know that it's really your own opinion fueling it.
stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Epictetus locate the source of suffering in the same place: not in what happens to you, but in what you believe about what happens to you.
action_step: Before your next stressful event today, take three slow breaths and remind yourself: I control how I show up. I don't control what happens next.
reflection_prompt: What would you do differently today if you genuinely didn't care whether you won or lost?
stoic_quote_note: Enchiridion text not available in public domain source files; quote sourced from modern translation.

sanskrit_line: karmaṇy evādhikāras te mā phaleṣu kadācana
translation: Thy right is to work only, but never with its fruits; let not the fruits of actions be thy motive, nor let thy attachment be to inaction.
in_plain_terms: This is probably the most famous verse in the Gita, and it's famous because it solves about half of your problems in one line. You get to do the work. You don't get to control the results. The second you start working for the outcome instead of the work itself, you've lost the thread.
stoic_parallel_quote: Externals are not in my power: will is in my power. Where shall I seek the good and the bad? Within, in the things which are my own.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus draw the same line: your jurisdiction ends at the action itself, and everything beyond that belongs to forces outside your control.
action_step: Pick one thing you've been agonizing over today. Do the part that's yours. Then put it down.
reflection_prompt: What are you holding onto right now that was never yours to carry?

sanskrit_line: yoga-sthaḥ kuru karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā dhanañjaya
translation: Perform action, O Arjuna, being steadfast in Yoga, abandoning attachment and balanced in success and failure! Evenness of mind is called Yoga.
in_plain_terms: Do the work. Drop the scoreboard. This isn't about being indifferent to quality. It's about not letting your mood swing with every win and loss. The athlete who checks the score every thirty seconds plays worse than the one who's just playing.
stoic_parallel_quote: Others again there be, who when they have done any such thing, do not so much as know what they have done; but are like unto the vine, which beareth her grapes, and when once she hath borne her own proper fruit, is contented and seeks for no further recompense.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus use the same image: the vine doesn't demand applause after bearing fruit. It just gets on with the next season.
action_step: For the next two hours, work without checking any metrics, likes, or feedback. Just do the work.
reflection_prompt: Where are you keeping score so obsessively that it's actually hurting your performance?

sanskrit_line: buddhi-yukto jahātīha ubhe sukṛta-duṣkṛte
translation: Endowed with wisdom (evenness of mind), one casts off in this life both good and evil deeds; therefore, devote thyself to Yoga; Yoga is skill in action.
in_plain_terms: 'Yoga is skill in action.' Three words that redefine what mastery means. It's not about what you achieve. It's about how you engage. The barista who's fully present while making your coffee is closer to this than the CEO checking email during a board meeting.
stoic_parallel_quote: The true joy of a man, is to do that which properly belongs unto a man.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus define the highest human achievement the same way: not results, but the quality of how you show up to the work.
action_step: Take your most boring task today and do it with total attention. Not because it deserves it, but because you do.
reflection_prompt: Where in your life are you performing with skill but without any presence?

sanskrit_line: prajahāti yadā kāmān sarvān pārtha mano-gatān
translation: When a man completely casts off, O Arjuna, all the desires of the mind and is satisfied in the Self by the Self, then is he said to be one of steady wisdom!
in_plain_terms: Real contentment isn't about getting everything on the list. It's about the moment you stop writing the list. You already have a version of everything you need. The gap between 'what I have' and 'what I want' is where most of your suffering lives.
stoic_parallel_quote: No person has the power to have everything they want, but it is in their power not to want what they don't have, and to cheerfully put to good use what they do have.
stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Seneca define contentment the same way: not getting everything you want, but cheerfully using what's already in front of you.
action_step: Look around the room you're in right now. Name three things you'd desperately miss if they were gone. You already have those.
reflection_prompt: What if the problem isn't that you don't have enough, but that you've never paused long enough to notice that you do?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: duḥkheṣv anudvigna-manāḥ sukheṣu vigata-spṛhaḥ
translation: He whose mind is not shaken by adversity, who does not hanker after pleasures, and who is free from attachment, fear and anger, is called a sage of steady wisdom.
in_plain_terms: Here's a checklist for how you're actually doing, and it's not about what you've accomplished. When things go wrong, does your mind stay steady? When something shiny shows up, do you lose your grip? Are fear and anger running your decisions? Be honest.
stoic_parallel_quote: Who then is the invincible? It is he whom none of the things disturb which are independent of the will.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus define strength the same way: not by what you can do, but by what can't knock you off center.
action_step: Right now, run a quick inner scan. On a scale of 1-10, how steady is your mind? Not happy. Steady. That's the metric that matters.
reflection_prompt: Which of these three has the most power over you right now: craving, fear, or anger?

sanskrit_line: dhyāyato viṣayān puṁsaḥ saṅgas teṣūpajāyate
translation: When a man thinks of the objects, attachment to them arises; from attachment desire is born; from desire anger arises.
in_plain_terms: This is the most precise map of a downward spiral ever drawn, and it's 2,500 years old. Thinking becomes attachment. Attachment becomes craving. Craving becomes anger when you can't have it. And once anger takes over, your judgment is gone. It's the blueprint for every doom-scroll and every 2 AM argument with someone who isn't in the room.
stoic_parallel_quote: Every habit and faculty is maintained and increased by the corresponding actions: the habit of walking by walking, the habit of running by running. If you would make anything a habit, do it; if you would not make it a habit, do not do it, but accustom yourself to do something else in place of it.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus show how mental patterns compound: the spiral goes one direction or the other, and the direction is set by what you keep practicing.
action_step: Notice the next time you pick up your phone without a reason. That's step one of the spiral. Just noticing it breaks the chain.
reflection_prompt: What thought have you been returning to obsessively this week, and where is it taking you?

sanskrit_line: rāga-dveṣa-vimuktais tu viṣayān indriyaiś caran
translation: But the self-controlled man, moving amongst objects with the senses under restraint, and free from attraction and repulsion, attains to peace.
in_plain_terms: You don't have to avoid the world to be at peace in it. The point isn't to stop feeling attraction or aversion. It's to stop letting those feelings make your decisions. You can walk through a bakery without buying everything. That's what self-control actually looks like.
stoic_parallel_quote: Whenever you get an impression of some pleasure, as with any impression, guard yourself from being carried away by it, let it await your action, give yourself a pause.
stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Epictetus teach the same skill: don't fight the feeling. Just insert a pause between the impulse and the action.
action_step: The next time you feel a strong pull toward something today, pause for five seconds. Don't fight it. Just insert a gap between the impulse and the action.
reflection_prompt: What's one thing you keep saying yes to automatically that you'd be better off pausing before?
stoic_quote_note: Enchiridion text not available in public domain source files; quote sourced from modern translation.

sanskrit_line: āpūryamāṇam acala-pratiṣṭhaṁ samudram āpaḥ praviśanti yadvat
translation: He attains peace into whom all desires enter as waters enter the ocean, which, filled from all sides, remains unmoved; but not the man who is full of desires.
in_plain_terms: Think about the ocean. Rivers pour into it constantly, and it doesn't overflow or panic. That's the model. Not blocking desires and distractions, but being large enough to absorb them without being moved. Your inbox, your notifications, your cravings. Let them arrive. You don't have to react to everything that shows up.
stoic_parallel_quote: It will but little avail thee, to turn thine anger and indignation upon the things themselves that have fallen cross unto thee. For as for them, they are not sensible of it.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus point out the same thing: the stuff that's bothering you isn't even aware that it's bothering you. Be large enough to absorb the impact.
action_step: For the next hour, let every notification arrive without immediately responding. Be the ocean, not the puddle.
reflection_prompt: What would it feel like to let things come to you without needing to react to all of them?

sanskrit_line: na karmaṇām anārambhān naiṣkarmyaṁ puruṣo 'śnute
translation: Not by the non-performance of actions does man reach actionlessness, nor by mere renunciation does he attain to perfection.
in_plain_terms: You can't think your way to transformation. At some point, the reading, the planning, the researching becomes a very sophisticated way of avoiding the work. You don't achieve anything by not doing things. You especially don't achieve freedom by sitting still and calling it enlightenment.
stoic_parallel_quote: Spend not the remnant of thy days in thoughts and fancies concerning other men, when it is not in relation to some common good, when by it thou art hindered from some other better work.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus deliver the same rebuke to the eternal student: stop spending your days on thoughts and fancies when the actual work is waiting.
action_step: What's the one thing you've been 'learning about' instead of actually doing? Take the smallest possible action on it right now.
reflection_prompt: Is there something you've turned into a permanent research project to avoid the discomfort of actually starting?

sanskrit_line: yas tv indriyāṇi manasā niyamyārabhate 'rjuna
translation: But whosoever, controlling the senses by the mind, O Arjuna, engages himself in Karma Yoga with the organs of action, without attachment, he excels!
in_plain_terms: The person who stays engaged with the world but keeps their inner compass steady beats the person who just checks out. Withdrawing from everything isn't strength. Showing up fully present, without getting dragged around by every desire? That's the harder and more valuable move.
stoic_parallel_quote: Were all the geniuses of history to focus on this single theme, they could never fully express their bafflement at the darkness of the human mind. No person would give up even an inch of their estate, and the slightest dispute with a neighbor can mean hell to pay; yet we easily let others encroach on our lives.
stoic_parallel_source: Seneca, On the Brevity of Life, 3.1-2
stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Seneca find it baffling how freely people surrender their time and attention while guarding everything else.
action_step: Do one thing you'd normally avoid today, but do it without complaining internally. That's the practice.
reflection_prompt: Are you confusing checking out with inner peace?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: tasmād asaktaḥ satataṁ kāryaṁ karma samācara
translation: Therefore, without attachment, do thou always perform action which should be done; for, by performing action without attachment man reaches the Supreme.
in_plain_terms: Show up. Do the work. Let go of the result. Repeat. Every day. That's it. This isn't some elevated spiritual state. It's an operating instruction for anyone who has a practice, a job, or a life they're trying to build. Motivation is nice when it shows up. It's not required.
stoic_parallel_quote: Be it all one unto thee, whether half frozen or well warm; whether only slumbering, or after a full sleep; whether discommended or commended thou do thy duty.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus say the same thing: do your duty whether you're cold, warm, praised, or criticized. The conditions don't get a vote.
action_step: Do the most important thing on your list today without checking how it's received. Ship it and move on.
reflection_prompt: If no one ever saw or acknowledged your work, would you still do it?

sanskrit_line: yad yad ācarati śreṣṭhas tat tad evetaro janaḥ
translation: Whatsoever a great man does, that other men also do; whatever he sets up as the standard, that the world follows.
in_plain_terms: People don't follow your advice. They follow your behavior. Your kids don't do what you tell them. They do what you do. Your team doesn't care about the values on the wall. They care about the values you demonstrate on a Tuesday afternoon when things go sideways.
stoic_parallel_quote: Whatsoever any man either doth or saith, thou must be good; not for any man's sake, but for thine own nature's sake; as if either gold, or the emerald, or purple, should ever be saying to themselves, Whatsoever any man either doth or saith, I must still be an emerald, and I must keep my colour.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus say the same thing: be the emerald. Your job is to show your true colors regardless of what anyone else does.
action_step: Think about one habit you want someone in your life to adopt. Now check: are you modeling it yourself?
reflection_prompt: If someone watched how you actually spend your time this week, what would they conclude your real priorities are?

sanskrit_line: prakṛteḥ kriyamāṇāni guṇaiḥ karmāṇi sarvaśaḥ
translation: All actions are wrought in all cases by the qualities of Nature only. He whose mind is deluded by egoism thinks: 'I am the doer'.
in_plain_terms: That big success you had? You worked hard, sure. But also: timing, luck, your parents, the economy, the person who introduced you, and about a thousand other things you had nothing to do with. Same goes for your failures. Taking full personal credit or blame for anything is just ego pretending it's running the show.
stoic_parallel_quote: Consider the whole universe whereof thou art but a very little part, and the whole age of the world together, whereof but a short and very momentary portion is allotted unto thee.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus use the vastness of the universe to shrink the ego. You're a participant in something enormous, not its sole author.
action_step: Think of your biggest recent win. Write down three things that contributed to it that had nothing to do with you.
reflection_prompt: Where are you taking too much credit or too much blame?

sanskrit_line: indriyasyendriyasyārthe rāga-dveṣau vyavasthitau
translation: Attachment and aversion for the objects of the senses abide in the senses; let none come under their sway, for they are his foes.
in_plain_terms: Your cravings aren't you. Neither are your aversions. They live in your nervous system, and they'll fire whether you want them to or not. The ice cream aisle will always call. The difficult email will always spike your heart rate. That's fine. You just don't have to obey.
stoic_parallel_quote: If thou canst but withdraw conceit and opinion concerning that which may seem hurtful and offensive, thou thyself art as safe, as safe may be.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus say the same thing: withdraw your opinion about the thing, and the thing loses its power over you.
action_step: Next time a craving hits, say to yourself: 'That's a signal, not a command.' Then wait 30 seconds.
reflection_prompt: What impulse have you been treating as part of your identity when it's really just a pattern?

sanskrit_line: śreyān sva-dharmo viguṇaḥ para-dharmāt sv-anuṣṭhitāt
translation: Better is one's own duty, though devoid of merit, than the duty of another well discharged. Better is death in one's own duty; the duty of another is fraught with fear.
in_plain_terms: Your messy, imperfect, genuine path is better than a flawless imitation of someone else's. This is the antidote to scrolling through other people's highlight reels and feeling like you're falling behind. You're not behind. You're in a different lane.
stoic_parallel_quote: Remember that you are an actor in a play, playing a character according to the will of the playwright. If he wishes you to play the beggar, play even that role well. For this is your duty, to perform well the character assigned you.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus say fulfillment comes from playing your own role well, not from wishing you'd been cast in someone else's.
action_step: Notice the next time you compare yourself to someone today. Then ask: would I actually want their entire life, not just the part I'm envying?
reflection_prompt: What would you be doing right now if you'd never seen anyone else's version of success?
stoic_quote_note: Enchiridion text not available in public domain source files; quote sourced from modern translation.

sanskrit_line: karmaṇy akarma yaḥ paśyed akarmaṇi ca karma yaḥ
translation: He who seeth inaction in action and action in inaction, he is wise among men; he is a Yogi and performer of all actions.
in_plain_terms: The busiest person in the room might be accomplishing nothing. The one sitting quietly might be doing the most important work of the day. Busyness isn't productivity, and rest isn't laziness. Sometimes the wisest action is to stop.
stoic_parallel_quote: The mind must be given relaxation. It will rise improved and sharper after a good break. Just as rich fields must not be forced, for they will quickly lose their fertility if never given a break.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca challenge the cult of busyness: real wisdom knows when stillness is more productive than motion.
action_step: Block 20 minutes of 'nothing' on your calendar today. No phone, no tasks. Just sit. See what happens.
reflection_prompt: Where in your life are you confusing being busy with making progress?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: yasya sarve samārambhāḥ kāma-saṅkalpa-varjitāḥ
translation: He whose undertakings are all devoid of desires and (selfish) purposes, and whose actions have been burnt by the fire of knowledge, him the wise call a sage.
in_plain_terms: When you strip away the self-interest from your work, something interesting happens. The quality goes up, not down. You're not performing for applause or angling for advantage. You're just doing what needs doing, cleanly, and moving on. The people who operate this way tend to be both the most effective and the most at peace.
stoic_parallel_quote: Wherever there is a human being, there is an opportunity for kindness.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca describe the same liberated actor: someone whose actions arise from wisdom and goodness rather than from wanting something back.
action_step: Do one genuinely kind thing today that no one will ever know about. Not for karma. Just because you can.
reflection_prompt: If you removed the need for recognition from your work, what would change about how you do it?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: tasmād ajñāna-sambhūtaṁ hṛt-sthaṁ jñānāsinātmanaḥ
translation: Therefore, with the sword of knowledge (of the Self) cut asunder the doubt of the self born of ignorance, residing in thy heart, and take refuge in Yoga; arise, O Arjuna!
in_plain_terms: You've done the research. You've gathered the opinions. You've thought about it from every angle. Now cut through it. The doubt that's left isn't wisdom. It's a habit. At some point, deliberation is just procrastination wearing a suit.
stoic_parallel_quote: But there is no reason to live and no limit to our miseries if we let our fears predominate.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca deliver the same ultimatum: at some point, continued deliberation is just fear with a better vocabulary.
action_step: That decision you've been sitting on? Make it in the next five minutes. Not perfectly. Just make it.
reflection_prompt: What doubt are you keeping alive because making a decision would mean you have to actually do something?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: brahmaṇy ādhāya karmāṇi saṅgaṁ tyaktvā karoti yaḥ
translation: He who performs actions, offering them to Brahman and abandoning attachment, is not tainted by sin as a lotus leaf by water.
in_plain_terms: The lotus grows in the mud but nothing sticks to it. That's the model. You don't need a clean environment to stay clean. You need the right relationship to the mess. Work in the chaos, help where it's needed, get your hands dirty. Just don't absorb it.
stoic_parallel_quote: Such as thy thoughts and ordinary cogitations are, such will thy mind be in time. For the soul doth as it were receive its tincture from the fancies, and imaginations.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita's lotus leaf and Marcus's counsel to guard your thoughts describe the same inner discipline: your surroundings don't determine your inner state. You do.
action_step: Think of one situation today where you're likely to absorb someone else's negativity. Decide in advance: picture the water sliding off the leaf.
reflection_prompt: What environment are you letting stain you that doesn't have to?

sanskrit_line: śaknotīhaiva yaḥ soḍhuṁ prāk śarīra-vimokṣaṇāt
translation: He who is able, while still here in this world to withstand, before the liberation from the body, the impulse born of desire and anger, he is a Yogi, he is a happy man.
in_plain_terms: Happiness isn't the absence of difficult feelings. It's the ability to withstand them without being wrecked. The person who can sit with desire without being dragged by it, and with anger without being consumed by it? That person is free in a way that has nothing to do with how their life looks from the outside.
stoic_parallel_quote: I may wish to be free from torture, but if the time comes for me to endure it, I'll wish to bear it courageously with bravery and honor. The point is not to wish for these adversities, but for the virtue that makes adversities bearable.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca redefine the good life: not the absence of difficulty, but the presence of the capacity to meet it without breaking.
action_step: The next time you feel a surge of anger or craving, try to sit with it for 60 seconds without acting on it. Just feel it. That's the whole practice.
reflection_prompt: What internal storm are you trying to eliminate when you should be learning to weather it?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: uddhared ātmanātmānaṁ nātmānam avasādayet
translation: Let a man lift himself by his own Self alone; let him not lower himself, for this self alone is the friend of oneself and this self alone is the enemy of oneself.
in_plain_terms: No one is coming to save you. That's not pessimism. That's the beginning of actual power. You're the only person who can pull yourself out of the ditch, and you're the only person who can push yourself into one. That might be the most terrifying and liberating thing you'll hear today.
stoic_parallel_quote: The material for the wise and good man is his own ruling faculty.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus identify you as both the raw material and the craftsman. You are the project and the worker at the same time.
action_step: Name one area where you've been waiting for someone else to fix things. Now name one tiny step you could take on it yourself. Take it.
reflection_prompt: In what part of your life are you waiting for rescue instead of rescuing yourself?

sanskrit_line: bandhur ātmātmanas tasya yenātmaivātmanā jitaḥ
translation: The self is the friend of the self for him who has conquered himself by the Self, but to the unconquered self, this self stands in the position of an enemy like the (external) foe.
in_plain_terms: Your relationship with yourself sets the terms for every other relationship you'll ever have. When your self-talk is destructive, you're at war with the one person you can never get away from. When you've made peace with yourself, you've secured the only ally who's guaranteed to show up every single day.
stoic_parallel_quote: I have often wondered how it should come to pass, that every man loving himself best, should more regard other men's opinions concerning himself than his own.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus point to the same paradox: we love ourselves above all others yet trust everyone else's opinion of us more than our own.
action_step: Catch the next negative thing you say to yourself and rephrase it the way you'd say it to a friend. Not cheerful. Just fair.
reflection_prompt: If your self-talk were a person, would you want them as a friend or would you cut them out of your life?

sanskrit_line: yuktāhāra-vihārasya yukta-ceṣṭasya karmasu
translation: Yoga becomes the destroyer of pain for him who is always moderate in eating and recreation (such as walking, etc.), who is moderate in exertion in actions, who is moderate in sleep and wakefulness.
in_plain_terms: Eat well. Sleep enough. Move your body. Don't overwork. This sounds boring because it is. But most 'spiritual practices' fail not because the person lacks willpower or enlightenment, but because they're skipping meals, sleeping four hours, and wondering why they can't focus. The basics aren't the foundation. They're the whole building.
stoic_parallel_quote: It is not the man who has too little that is poor, but the one who hankers after more.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca insist on the same unglamorous truth: more isn't better. Moderation in the basics is the foundation everything else is built on.
action_step: Tonight, go to bed 30 minutes earlier than usual. That's it. That's the spiritual practice.
reflection_prompt: Which fundamental are you neglecting right now: sleep, food, movement, or rest?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: yato yato niścalati manaś cañcalam asthiram
translation: From whatever cause the restless, unsteady mind wanders away, from that let him restrain it and bring it under the control of the Self alone.
in_plain_terms: Your mind wanders. You bring it back. That's not the failure of meditation. That IS meditation. Every time you notice you've drifted and return to focus, you just did a rep. The person whose mind never wanders isn't more advanced. They're probably just not paying attention.
stoic_parallel_quote: This is the true athlete, the man who exercises himself against such appearances. Stay, wretch, do not be carried away. Great is the combat, divine is the work; it is for kingship, for freedom, for happiness, for freedom from perturbation.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus frame mental discipline as an athletic event. The struggle isn't a sign of failure. It's the exercise through which mastery is built.
action_step: Set a timer for two minutes. Focus on your breath. When your mind wanders (it will), just bring it back. Each return is a successful rep.
reflection_prompt: Where else in your life do you confuse the practice with the failure?

sanskrit_line: cañcalaṁ hi manaḥ kṛṣṇa pramāthi balavad dṛḍham
translation: The mind verily is restless, turbulent, strong and unyielding, O Krishna! I deem it as difficult to control as to control the wind.
in_plain_terms: If it makes you feel any better, Arjuna was the greatest warrior of his generation and he said exactly what you're thinking right now: 'I can't control my own mind, and honestly, trying feels like trying to control the wind.' You're not uniquely bad at focus. Your mind is designed to wander. Everyone's is.
stoic_parallel_quote: Let it be thy earnest and incessant care to perform whatsoever it is that thou art about, with true and unfeigned gravity, natural affection, freedom and justice: and as for all other cares and imaginations, how thou mayest ease thy mind of them.
stoic_bridge: Both Arjuna and Marcus acknowledge the mind's restlessness as universal. Both insist that acknowledging the difficulty is the first step, not an excuse to quit.
action_step: The next time your mind races during an important task, don't fight it. Just say 'I see you,' and gently redirect. No judgment.
reflection_prompt: What if the difficulty of controlling your mind isn't a personal failing but just the nature of having a mind?

sanskrit_line: asaṁśayaṁ mahā-bāho mano durnigrahaṁ calam
translation: Undoubtedly, O mighty-armed Arjuna, the mind is difficult to control and restless; but, by practice and by dispassion it may be restrained!
in_plain_terms: Krishna doesn't deny it. 'Yeah, the mind is hard to control. No question.' But then comes the answer: practice and dispassion. Two levers. Practice means you keep showing up. Dispassion means you stop caring so much about the results of that practice. Both are learnable. Neither comes naturally.
stoic_parallel_quote: But what is philosophizing? Is it not a preparation against events which may happen?
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus respond to 'this is too hard' with the same honesty: of course it's hard. What's the alternative? The difficulty is the point.
action_step: Pick one small mental discipline you've abandoned (meditation, journaling, whatever). Do it for two minutes today. Just two.
reflection_prompt: What have you quit because it was hard, even though you knew the difficulty was the point?

sanskrit_line: daivī hy eṣā guṇa-mayī mama māyā duratyayā
translation: Verily this divine illusion of Mine made up of the qualities (of Nature) is difficult to cross over; those who take refuge in Me alone cross over this illusion.
in_plain_terms: The patterns that keep you asleep aren't obvious. They're woven into everything: your habits, your assumptions, the things you've never questioned because they feel like 'just the way things are.' Seeing through them is hard. The Gita doesn't pretend otherwise. But it's possible. It just takes sustained attention, not a one-time revelation.
stoic_parallel_quote: Circumstances are what deceive us. You must be discerning in them. We embrace evil before good. We desire the opposite of what we once desired.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca acknowledge that the forces keeping us asleep are woven into experience itself. Overcoming them takes sustained discernment, not willpower alone.
action_step: Pick one belief you hold about yourself ('I'm not creative,' 'I'm bad with money') and ask: where did I actually get this? Is it still true?
reflection_prompt: What assumption about your life have you never questioned because it's been there so long it feels like a fact?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: yaṁ yaṁ vāpi smaran bhāvaṁ tyajaty ante kalevaram
translation: Whosoever at the end leaves the body, thinking of any being, to that being only does he go, O son of Kunti (Arjuna), because of his constant thought of that being!
in_plain_terms: You become what you think about. Not what you think about once, but what you return to again and again. Your mind is being dyed by whatever you keep exposing it to. Your news feed, your conversations, your late-night worries. These aren't neutral. They're forming you.
stoic_parallel_quote: Such as thy thoughts and ordinary cogitations are, such will thy mind be in time. For the soul doth as it were receive its tincture from the fancies, and imaginations.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus say the same thing: your mind takes the shape of what it frequently holds. Choose carefully, because this isn't theoretical.
action_step: Do a quick audit of the last three things you consumed on your phone. Did they move you toward the person you want to be, or away?
reflection_prompt: If your habitual thoughts were shaping your future right now, where would you be heading?

sanskrit_line: kālo 'smi loka-kṣaya-kṛt pravṛddho lokān samāhartum iha pravṛttaḥ
translation: I am the mighty world-destroying Time, now engaged in destroying the worlds. Even without thee, none of the warriors arrayed in the hostile armies shall live.
in_plain_terms: This is the cosmic cold shower. 'I am Time, the destroyer of worlds.' Oppenheimer quoted it, and it still hits. Everything you're worried about today, your reputation, your performance review, that argument, none of it survives the long game. That's not depressing. It's freeing. If none of it lasts, you might as well stop clutching it so tightly.
stoic_parallel_quote: Alexander of Macedon, and he that dressed his mules, when once dead both came to one.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus use death to dissolve the illusion that status or achievement can be made permanent. Alexander and his mule-keeper end up in the same place.
action_step: Think of the thing consuming most of your mental energy today. Now fast-forward 100 years. Notice how the grip loosens.
reflection_prompt: What would you stop caring about if you truly believed none of this was permanent?

sanskrit_line: tasmāt tvam uttiṣṭha yaśo labhasva jitvā śatrūn bhuṅkṣva rājyaṁ samṛddham
translation: Therefore, stand up and obtain fame. Conquer the enemies and enjoy the unrivalled kingdom. Verily, they have already been slain by Me; be thou a mere instrument, O Arjuna!
in_plain_terms: Stop agonizing. The outcome is already in motion. You're not the author of what happens next. You're the instrument. Your only job is to stand up and act. This is the most galvanizing verse in the Gita: it doesn't ask you to figure everything out. It just asks you to show up.
stoic_parallel_quote: It is circumstances which show what men are. Therefore when a difficulty falls upon you, remember that God, like a trainer of wrestlers, has matched you with a rough young man. For what purpose? Why, that you may become an Olympic conqueror; but it is not accomplished without sweat.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus reframe difficulty the same way: you're not being punished by what's being asked of you. You're being trained. Your job is to rise to it.
action_step: Identify one thing you've been overthinking. Stand up (literally, if you can) and take the first physical action toward it.
reflection_prompt: What are you waiting for permission to do that you already know you need to start?

sanskrit_line: śreyo hi jñānam abhyāsāj jñānād dhyānaṁ viśiṣyate
translation: Better indeed is knowledge than practice; than knowledge meditation is better; than meditation the renunciation of the fruits of actions; peace immediately follows renunciation.
in_plain_terms: If you can only do one thing, do this: let go of results. The Gita ranks all the spiritual practices and puts this one at the top. Not meditation, not study, not discipline. Just releasing your grip on how things turn out. Peace follows immediately. Not eventually. Immediately.
stoic_parallel_quote: Don't seek for everything to happen as you wish it would, but rather wish that everything happens as it actually will, then your life will flow well.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus arrive at the same destination: stop insisting things should be different from what they are, and peace follows immediately.
action_step: Pick one thing you're anxiously awaiting the outcome of. Imagine the worst version. Now the best. Notice: you're okay in both. Let go.
reflection_prompt: What result are you holding onto so tightly that the holding itself has become the source of your suffering?
stoic_quote_note: Enchiridion text not available in public domain source files; quote sourced from modern translation.

sanskrit_line: adveṣṭā sarva-bhūtānāṁ maitraḥ karuṇa eva ca
translation: He who hates no creature, who is friendly and compassionate to all, who is free from attachment and egoism, balanced in pleasure and pain, and forgiving,
in_plain_terms: This is a character checklist, and it's worth going through honestly. Do you hate anyone? Are you genuinely friendly, not just polite? Free from ego, or just good at hiding it? Can you handle pleasure and pain without either one running you? Are you forgiving? Not as a theory. Actually forgiving. Right now.
stoic_parallel_quote: Make it not any longer a matter of dispute or discourse, what are the signs and proprieties of a good man, but really and actually to be such.
stoic_bridge: The Gita gives the detailed checklist. Marcus gives the summary: stop debating what a good person looks like and be one.
action_step: Pick one quality from this list that you're weakest on today. Don't try to fix it. Just notice it. Awareness comes first.
reflection_prompt: If you were graded on friendliness, humility, and forgiveness today, what would the report card look like?

sanskrit_line: yasmān nodvijate loko lokān nodvijate ca yaḥ
translation: He by whom the world is not agitated and who cannot be agitated by the world, and who is freed from joy, envy, fear and anxiety, he is dear to Me.
in_plain_terms: Here's a simple test: do people tense up when you enter the room, or relax? And when the room gets tense, do you absorb it or stay steady? The ideal is someone who neither creates agitation nor catches it. That's a rare person and a remarkably useful one.
stoic_parallel_quote: Will any contemn me? let him look to that, upon what grounds he does it: my care shall be that I may never be found either doing or speaking anything that doth truly deserve contempt.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus say the same thing: manage your own behavior and let the world's opinion of you be the world's problem.
action_step: In your next interaction today, notice whether you're adding calm or adding static. Don't judge it. Just observe.
reflection_prompt: Does the world agitate you more, or do you agitate the world more?

sanskrit_line: samaḥ śatrau ca mitre ca tathā mānāpamānayoḥ
translation: He who is the same to foe and friend, and in honour and dishonour, who is the same in cold and heat and in pleasure and pain, who is free from attachment,
in_plain_terms: Same toward your enemy and your friend. Same when people praise you and when they tear you apart. Same in comfort and pain. This sounds superhuman, and honestly, it kind of is. But it's the goal. Not because feelings don't matter, but because consistency does. The person who's steady in all conditions is the one you trust with everything.
stoic_parallel_quote: He must adapt his wish to what is going on, so that nothing takes place contrary to his wish, and nothing that he wishes for fails to take place.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus describe the same aspiration: a person so aligned with reality that no circumstance can dislodge their composure.
action_step: Think of someone who annoys you. Now think of someone you love. Try, just for a moment, to wish them both well equally. Notice how hard it is.
reflection_prompt: Where does your behavior change most dramatically depending on who's watching?

sanskrit_line: kṣetra-kṣetrajñayor evam antaraṁ jñāna-cakṣuṣā
translation: Just as the one sun illumines the whole world, so also the Lord of the Field (the Supreme Self) illumines the whole Field, O Arjuna!
in_plain_terms: You are not your thoughts. You are not your body. You are not your circumstances. You're the awareness watching all of it. That sounds abstract until you've had even one moment of watching your own mind panic while some deeper part of you stays perfectly calm. That deeper part is what this verse is pointing at.
stoic_parallel_quote: This day I did come out of all my trouble. Nay I have cast out all my trouble; for that which troubled thee, was not without anywhere that thou shouldest come out of it, but within in thine own opinions, from whence it must be cast out, before thou canst truly and constantly be at ease.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus locate freedom in the same realization: the trouble is never outside you. It's in your opinions about what's outside you.
action_step: Close your eyes for 30 seconds. Watch your thoughts like you'd watch cars passing on a highway. You're not the cars. You're the one watching.
reflection_prompt: What would change if you stopped identifying with your thoughts and started watching them instead?

sanskrit_line: abhayaṁ sattva-saṁśuddhir jñāna-yoga-vyavasthitiḥ
translation: Fearlessness, purity of heart, steadfastness in Yoga and knowledge, alms-giving, control of the senses, sacrifice, study of scriptures, austerity and straightforwardness,
in_plain_terms: Fearlessness leads the list. Not courage, which implies you feel the fear and push through. Fearlessness. As in: you've done enough inner work that the fear itself has lost its grip. It's listed before purity, before discipline, before knowledge. Because without it, none of the other qualities can fully show up.
stoic_parallel_quote: Do you then consider that the chief of all evils to man and the chief mark of mean spirit and of cowardice is not death, but rather the fear of death? Against this fear then I advise you to exercise yourself, and you will know that thus only are men made free.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Epictetus put fearlessness at the foundation of all virtue. Without it, everything else is just decoration.
action_step: Identify one thing you're afraid of doing today. It doesn't have to be huge. Send the message, ask the question, make the call.
reflection_prompt: What quality on this list (fearlessness, purity, steadfastness, generosity, self-control, honesty) are you most neglecting right now?

sanskrit_line: tri-vidhaṁ narakasyedaṁ dvāraṁ nāśanam ātmanaḥ
translation: Triple is the gate of this hell, destructive of the self, lust, anger, and greed, therefore, one should abandon these three.
in_plain_terms: Three gates lead to self-destruction: lust, anger, and greed. That's it. Not a complicated taxonomy. Just three things. Before you make any important decision, run it through the filter: is lust, anger, or greed driving this? If the answer is yes to any of those, stop. Walk it off. Decide later.
stoic_parallel_quote: Meekness is a thing unconquerable, if it be true and natural, and not affected or hypocritical.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus identify anger as a gate to self-destruction and redefine strength: real power is the capacity for calm when rage is calling.
action_step: Run your current biggest source of stress through the three-gate test: is desire, anger, or greed driving your reaction? Name it honestly.
reflection_prompt: Which of the three gates (desire, anger, greed) has the most influence over your decisions right now?

sanskrit_line: yat tad agre viṣam iva pariṇāme 'mṛtopamam
translation: That which is like poison at first but in the end like nectar, that pleasure is declared to be Sattwic, born of the purity of one's own mind due to Self-realisation.
in_plain_terms: The workout is miserable for the first ten minutes. The meditation feels pointless for the first two weeks. The difficult conversation is awful in the moment. But afterward, every single one of these leaves you better than you found yourself. Meanwhile, the easy shortcut tastes great going down and terrible sitting in your stomach. Choose your poison. Literally.
stoic_parallel_quote: Success comes to the lowly and to the poorly talented, but the special characteristic of a great person is to triumph over the disasters and panics of human life.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Seneca affirm that difficulty at the beginning is not a sign of a wrong path. Greatness is forged by enduring what feels hard until the reward appears.
action_step: Do one thing today that feels unpleasant but you know is good for you. Start the workout, have the conversation, clean the thing. The nectar is on the other side.
reflection_prompt: What 'poison' have you been avoiding that you know would eventually become nectar?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: śreyān sva-dharmo viguṇaḥ para-dharmāt sv-anuṣṭhitāt
translation: Better is one's own duty (though) destitute of merits, than the duty of another well performed. He who does the duty ordained by his own nature incurs no sin.
in_plain_terms: This verse appears twice in the Gita. That's how much it matters. Your clumsy, imperfect, authentic work beats a polished imitation of someone else's path. Every single time. Stop looking at what your neighbor is building and build the thing that's yours.
stoic_parallel_quote: What is there remaining that should be dear unto thee? This I think: that in all thy motions and actions thou be moved, and restrained according to thine own true natural constitution and construction only.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus say the same thing: prize your own nature, your own path. Harmony comes from honoring your own duty, not from imitating another's.
action_step: Unfollow or mute one account that makes you feel like you should be living a different life.
reflection_prompt: What would you build if you stopped looking at what everyone else is building?

sanskrit_line: saha-jaṁ karma kaunteya sa-doṣam api na tyajet
translation: One should not abandon, O Arjuna, the duty to which one is born, though faulty; for, all undertakings are enveloped by evil, as fire by smoke!
in_plain_terms: Every fire produces smoke. Every project has problems. Every person has flaws. If you're waiting for a perfect, clean version of the thing you need to do, you'll wait forever. Imperfection isn't a bug. It's a feature of being alive. Start anyway.
stoic_parallel_quote: Not as though thou hadst thousands of years to live. Death hangs over thee: whilst yet thou livest, whilst thou mayest, be good.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus cut through perfectionism with the same urgency: you'll never do it perfectly, and you're running out of time. Start now.
action_step: Ship that thing you've been polishing. Send the email you've been rewriting. Post the work you've been holding back. It's good enough.
reflection_prompt: What have you been refusing to start because you can't guarantee it'll be perfect?

sanskrit_line: iti te jñānam ākhyātaṁ guhyād guhyataraṁ mayā
translation: Thus has wisdom more secret than secrecy itself been declared unto thee by Me; having reflected over it fully, then act thou as thou wishest.
in_plain_terms: After 18 chapters of the most profound spiritual teaching ever delivered, Krishna's last word is: 'Now decide for yourself.' No ultimatum. No threat. Just, 'I've told you what I know. Think about it. Do what you think is right.' That's the deepest respect anyone can show you.
stoic_parallel_quote: Show me that the good life doesn't consist in its length, but in its use, and that it is possible, no, entirely too common, for a person who has had a long life to have lived too little.
stoic_bridge: Both Krishna and Seneca treat you as a free agent: wisdom is offered freely, but the decision of how to live is yours alone.
action_step: Think of the last piece of advice you received that felt right. Stop waiting for more information. Act on it today.
reflection_prompt: What do you already know that you're pretending you still need to research?
stoic_quote_note: Seneca quote from verses-100.json; pending verification against public domain Seneca source.

sanskrit_line: sarva-dharmān parityajya mām ekaṁ śaraṇaṁ vraja
translation: Abandoning all duties, take refuge in Me alone; I will liberate thee from all sins; grieve not.
in_plain_terms: After all the analysis, all the practice, all the frameworks, sometimes you just have to let go. Not give up. Let go. Trust that the effort you've put in has done its work and release your grip on the steering wheel. This isn't passivity. It's the final act of someone who's done everything within their power and is now ready to let the rest unfold.
stoic_parallel_quote: To suffer change can be no hurt; as no benefit it is, by change to attain to being.
stoic_bridge: Both the Gita and Marcus arrive at the same place: release all fixed positions. There's no harm in change, and no lasting safety in clinging.
action_step: Name one thing you've been controlling obsessively. Hold it loosely for the rest of the day. See what happens.
reflection_prompt: What would it feel like to trust that you've done enough?
